# 🛡️ Antivirus False Positives — What to do if Windows Defender flags LocalPDF Hub

If Windows Defender (or another antivirus) flagged `LocalPDF-Hub.exe` as a virus — usually with a name like **`Trojan:Win32/Wacatac.B!ml`** or similar — **don't panic**. This is a well-known false positive that affects many legitimate indie apps, and this page explains exactly why it happens and how to safely allow the program.

---

## 🔍 The short answer

**LocalPDF Hub contains no malware.** The detection is a false positive generated by the heuristic engine of Windows Defender, not by an actual signature of malicious code. The app:

- Makes **zero outbound network connections** (it doesn't even connect to the internet to check for updates)
- Does **not read files outside the folders you explicitly choose**
- Does **not modify the Windows registry**
- Does **not install anything** — it's a single portable `.exe`

You can verify this yourself by inspecting the network activity with tools like Wireshark or TCPView — you'll see the app only listens on `127.0.0.1:8000` (your own machine) and makes no outbound calls.

---

## 🤔 Why does Windows flag it?

The detection name ends in **`.B!ml`** — the `ml` suffix stands for **"machine learning"**. This means Windows Defender's ML model flagged the file as *potentially suspicious* based on patterns, **not** because it matches any known malware signature.

There are three well-documented reasons why this happens to apps like LocalPDF Hub:

### 1. It's packaged with PyInstaller

LocalPDF Hub is built with Python and compiled into a single `.exe` using **PyInstaller**. PyInstaller works by bundling the Python interpreter, all libraries and your code into a self-extracting executable. This bundling pattern is legitimate and used by thousands of apps — but **malware authors also abuse PyInstaller** because it's easy to use. So antivirus ML models learned to be suspicious of any PyInstaller output, regardless of what's inside.

This is a known issue tracked by the PyInstaller project itself:  
https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/issues/5854

### 2. It's not signed with a commercial certificate

Windows trusts executables that are signed with a **code-signing certificate** issued by a Certificate Authority like DigiCert or Sectigo. These certificates cost **between $200 and $500 USD per year** — a reasonable price for a company, but prohibitive for an indie developer distributing a free app.

Without that certificate, Windows treats the `.exe` as "software from an unknown publisher" and applies stricter heuristics. Over time, as more users download the file and nothing bad happens, Microsoft's reputation system (SmartScreen) starts to trust it more — but the first hundred downloads will always trigger warnings.

### 3. The app acts as a local server

LocalPDF Hub starts a FastAPI/Uvicorn server on `127.0.0.1:8000` (your own machine, not the internet). This pattern — an app that listens on a network port — is also a pattern used by some malware for command-and-control. Again: legitimate use, but it matches the ML training data for "suspicious behavior".

---

## ✅ How to verify for yourself (before trusting our word)

**You don't have to take our word for it.** Here's how you can independently verify the file is clean:

### Option 1 — Upload to VirusTotal

1. Go to https://www.virustotal.com
2. Upload `LocalPDF-Hub.exe`
3. You'll see the file scanned by **70+ antivirus engines** simultaneously

A real malware file typically gets flagged by 30-50+ engines. A false positive like this one usually gets flagged by only 2-5 heuristic engines (Windows Defender being one of them), while the majority report it as clean. If you see this pattern, it's a false positive.

### Option 2 — Verify the SHA256 checksum

Every release on GitHub has an automatically-generated SHA256 checksum. You can verify the file you downloaded matches the one published:

```powershell
# In PowerShell:
Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 LocalPDF-Hub.exe
```

Compare the output with the checksum shown on the [Releases page](https://github.com/alaancuevas/LocalPDF-Hub-releases/releases/latest). If they match, the file hasn't been tampered with.

### Option 3 — Inspect the network activity

Download [TCPView](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/tcpview) from Microsoft Sysinternals (it's free and official). Run LocalPDF Hub and watch TCPView: you'll see a single entry for `LocalPDF-Hub.exe` listening on `127.0.0.1:8000` and **nothing else**. No outbound connections, ever.

---

## 🔧 How to allow the app on Windows 10 / 11

If you've verified the file is safe and want to run it, here's how to tell Windows Defender to allow it.

### Method 1 — Restore from quarantine (recommended)

If Windows Defender has already quarantined the file, you can restore it:

1. Press `Win` and type **"Windows Security"**, open the app.
2. Click **Virus & threat protection**.
3. Under "Current threats", click **Protection history**.
4. Find the entry for `LocalPDF-Hub.exe` (it will say "Threat blocked" or "Quarantined").
5. Click on it to expand the details.
6. Click **Actions** → **Allow on device**.
7. Confirm. The file is now restored and can run normally.

### Method 2 — Add an exclusion before downloading

If you want to prevent Windows from flagging the file in the first place:

1. Press `Win` and type **"Windows Security"**, open the app.
2. Click **Virus & threat protection**.
3. Under "Virus & threat protection settings", click **Manage settings**.
4. Scroll to the bottom and under "Exclusions", click **Add or remove exclusions**.
5. Click **Add an exclusion** → **Folder**.
6. Select the folder where you plan to save `LocalPDF-Hub.exe` (for example, `C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\LocalPDF-Hub\`).
7. Confirm. Files in that folder will no longer be scanned.

> ⚠️ **Security tip:** only exclude a specific folder (not your entire Downloads directory), and only use this method for files you've verified are safe. Don't make blanket exclusions.

### Method 3 — Temporarily disable real-time protection

**Not recommended for most users**, but if the above methods don't work:

1. Open **Windows Security** → **Virus & threat protection** → **Manage settings**.
2. Toggle **Real-time protection** to Off.
3. Download and run LocalPDF Hub.
4. Turn real-time protection back on immediately after.

Windows will automatically re-enable real-time protection after a while even if you forget.

---

## 🙋 Still not sure?

If you're not 100% comfortable running the file, **that's completely valid**. You have several options:

- **Wait**: As more users download the app, Microsoft's SmartScreen reputation grows and the warnings disappear on their own. This typically happens within a few weeks of each release.
- **Use a sandbox**: Run LocalPDF Hub inside **Windows Sandbox** (a built-in isolation feature on Windows 10/11 Pro) or a virtual machine. This way even if something were wrong, it wouldn't affect your main system.
- **Open an issue**: If you have questions or found something that seems genuinely suspicious, [open an issue](https://github.com/alaancuevas/LocalPDF-Hub-releases/issues) and I'll respond.

---

## 📚 Further reading

For technical users interested in the topic of antivirus false positives on indie software:

- Microsoft's own guidance on reporting false positives: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/wdsi/filesubmission
- PyInstaller's FAQ on antivirus detections: https://pyinstaller.org/en/stable/when-things-go-wrong.html#treating-binaries-as-malware
- General discussion on code signing for indie devs: https://melatonin.dev/blog/code-signing-on-windows-with-azure-trusted-signing/

---

**LocalPDF Hub is free, private, and open about how it works. If you still have doubts after reading this, that's fine — feel free to not use the app. Your trust matters more than any download.**
